---
title: "Evaluation Design and Its Components"
description: "Study note on Evaluation Design and Its Components in Practical Program Evaluation."
book_id: "practical-program-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Major section"
source_pages: "39-40"
citation_key: "chen_2015_practical_program_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:practical-program-evaluation:ch01-evaluation-design-and-its-components:c0002"
source_status: "needs_review"
---
## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2015</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Source pages 39-40</span><span>Major section</span></div>
This note summarises the MCP research-library section "Evaluation Design and Its Components" from Huey T. Chen's *Practical Program Evaluation* [@chen_2015_practical_program_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

## Core Argument

The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.

## Study Summary

The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.

A useful design does more than select methods. It asks whether the program is described coherently enough to support evaluation and whether stakeholders need a logic model, a fuller program theory, or both.

The practical lesson is proportionality: choose enough structure to make the evaluation credible and useful without forcing every program into the same template.

## Key Concepts

- Evaluation purpose
- Program description
- Method fit
- Stakeholder agreement

## Source Location

- Section path: Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation > Evaluation Design and Its Components
- Evidence records: `chunk:practical-program-evaluation:ch01-evaluation-design-and-its-components:c0002`

## Practical Implications

- Do not move to methods before the program description is adequate.
- Use design conversations to surface what stakeholders expect the evaluation to decide.
- Escalate from a logic model to program theory when context or causal mechanisms need to be examined.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Concepts: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Map: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What is the main evaluation problem that Evaluation Design and Its Components helps diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this guidance in a live evaluation?
3. How would this change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, or reporting?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## References
